statement.markdown:
Given the root of a binary search tree (BST) with N nodes and two node values p and q, find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) value of the two nodes in the BST.

The lowest common ancestor is defined between two nodes p and q as the lowest node in the BST that has both p and q as descendants (where a descendant may be a node or an edge). A node is a descendant of another node if the other node is on the path from the root to this node.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer N, the number of nodes in the BST.
The next N lines contain node values and their children, in the format: 'value left_child right_child' where left_child and right_child are the 0-based indices of the left and right child nodes respectively, or -1 if the child doesn't exist.
The next line contains two integers p and q representing the values of the two target nodes.
statement.outputFormat: The value of the LCA node.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the BST.

examples.explanation: The LCA of nodes 2 and 8 is 6.
The LCA of nodes 2 and 4 is 2.

hints.text: The problem involves a binary search tree (BST) and finding the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of two nodes. Recall that a BST has the property that for any node, all elements in its left subtree are less than the node, and all elements in its right subtree are greater than the node.

The LCA of two nodes in a BST is the node that is furthest from the root and is an ancestor of both nodes. Given the properties of a BST, if the two nodes are in different subtrees of a node, that node could be the LCA. Consider how the BST property can help in narrowing down the search for the LCA.

Since the BST property implies that all nodes to the left of a given node have values less than the node, and all nodes to the right have values greater than the node, you can use this property to guide your search for the LCA. Specifically, consider starting at the root and traversing down the tree based on the values of the two nodes.

The key insight is to realize that you can find the LCA by traversing the BST from the root, making decisions based on the values of the two nodes relative to the current node. If one node is in the left subtree and the other in the right subtree of the current node, the current node is the LCA. If both nodes are in the left subtree, continue left. If both are in the right subtree, continue right.

editorial.title: Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Search Tree
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by traversing the binary search tree from the root node, moving left if both p and q are less than the current node's value, and moving right if both p and q are greater than the current node's value. This process continues until we find a node where p and q are on different sides of the node, which is the lowest common ancestor.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve finding the paths from the root to both p and q, and then finding the last common node in these paths. However, this approach is slow due to the overhead of finding the paths and comparing them, resulting in a higher time complexity.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use a recursive or iterative approach to find the lowest common ancestor, taking advantage of the properties of a binary search tree to reduce the search space.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to traverse the tree iteratively, moving left or right based on the comparison of p and q with the current node's value, resulting in a time complexity of O(H), where H is the height of the tree.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that the algorithm always moves towards the lowest common ancestor of p and q, and stops when it finds a node that is the lowest common ancestor. This is because the algorithm moves left if both p and q are less than the current node's value, and moves right if both p and q are greater than the current node's value, ensuring that it never moves away from the lowest common ancestor.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm can be performed by manually tracing the execution of the algorithm on a sample input, verifying that it produces the correct output and has the expected time complexity.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where p or q is not present in the tree
Not correctly updating the current node during traversal
Not stopping the traversal when the lowest common ancestor is found
Not considering the properties of a binary search tree to reduce the search space
Using a recursive approach without considering the risk of stack overflow for large inputs

functionSignature.cpp: int lowestCommonAncestor(TreeNode* root, int p, int q);
functionSignature.java: public int lowestCommonAncestor(TreeNode root, int p, int q);
functionSignature.python: def lowestCommonAncestor(self, root: TreeNode, p: int, q: int) -> int:
